title: Abstract
content: Ru doped anatase supported on silica was prepared by solid-state dispersion method and examined for the photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide in aqueous medium at ambient conditions.To assist in interpreting the photocatalytic behaviour of Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 , reference systems consisting of Ru doped TiO 2 and TiO 2 supported on SiO 2 were also analyzed and the conditions were optimized.RuTiO 2 photocatalysts with metal loadings of 0.1, 0.3, 0.5 and 1.0 wt were prepared by impregnation method and a series of TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts with low TiO 2 (1, 3, 5 and 10 wt) contents were prepared by solid-state dispersion method.The photocatalysts were characterized using Xray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV-vis DRS, FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy.The results showed that TiO 2 particles with Ru ions have higher photocatalytic activity than undoped TiO 2 and the optimum Ru loading was found to be 0.5 wt.Nevertheless, the yield increased notably when TiO 2 was supported on SiO 2 .This strong enhancement suggests that in 10 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 the efficiency of charge separation is strongly influenced through the presence of Ti-O-Si bridging bonds.On the contrary, Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 has no significant improvement in activity over TiO 2 SiO 2 except that it shows nearly quadruple times higher activity for the formation of methanol than RuTiO 2 .The difference in the photocatalytic activity is related to its physico-chemical properties.

title: Experimental
content: Ruthenium trichloride trihydrate, 2-propanol and tetraethyl orthosilicate were of Analar grade purchased from Merck, Germany.TiO 2 (99 pure) was purchased from Lancaster and was used as such without any further purification for all the studies. RuTiO 2 samples with Ru loading ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 (ww) using TiO 2 was prepared by incipient wet impregnation method 38 with an aqueous solution of ruthenium trichloride trihydrate.An appropriate amount of the precursor, so as to obtain the desired Ru concentration in TiO 2 , was mixed with TiO 2 in triply distilled water.The resulting slurry was subjected to continuous stirring at ambient temperature, and subsequently dried in an oven at 383 K for 10 h.The solid residue was then crushed and calcined in air for 5 h followed by reduction in hydrogen atmosphere at 673 K for 6 h.The prepared catalysts thus obtained were stored in vacuum desiccator. TiO 2 and RuTiO 2 were supported on SiO 2 , obtained by the hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate in the presence of glacial acetic acid 39.In order to establish a trend in photocatalytic activity, TiO 2 SiO 2 and Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 were prepared by solid-state dispersion method to yield photocatalysts of similar properties, such as specific surface area, particle size, amount of surface hydroxyl groups and defects or sites acting as recombination centers of TiO 2 and Ru TiO 2 .TiO 2 SiO 2 photocatalysts with loadings of 1, 3, 5 and 10 wt were prepared by mixing appropriate amount of TiO 2 and SiO 2 thoroughly in ethanol and dried at 110 8C followed by calcination in air at 450 8C for 6 h 40. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of RuTiO 2 and TiO 2 SiO 2 were obtained on a Rich Seifert X-ray diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation (l 1.5406A ) over the scan range of 2u between 20 and 808 at 295 K, in order to identify the phase present.The catalysts were pressured inside the sample holder, and the XRD data were collected in the step scan mode. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) observed the morphological and structural differences of all the catalysts.Catalyst grains were dispersed in methanol and specimen stub was dipped into the liquid and dried.The specimen was then coated with gold using an ion sputter coater and examined under Leica Cambridge Steroscan 440 model coupled with an energy dispersive spectroscopy accessory (EDX). The topography and particle size of RuTiO 2 was measured using JEOL JEM-2000FX transmission electron microscopy (TEM) working at 200 kV.Prior to the analysis, the catalyst sample was ground into powder (using mortar and pestle) and then ultrasonically dispersed in a solvent and placed on a carbon-coated copper grid.The sample was allowed to dry before TEM analysis. Diffuse reflectance spectra of all the photocatalysts were recorded on a Shimadzu 2100 UV-vis spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200-800 nm equipped with an integrating sphere.Prior to the analysis, the background reflectance of BaSO 4 was measured and then the samples were spread onto BaSO 4 plates for analysis.The reflectance was converted to F(R 1 ) values by the instrument software using Kubelka-Munk function 41, where k and s are absorption and scattering coefficients, respectively, and R is the reflectance at the front face.The band gap was obtained from a plot of modified Kubelka-Munk function F(R 1 )E 12 versus energy of exciting light by converting the scanning wavelength (l) into photon energies using the relationship of E g 1240l.The vibrational and electronic transitions occurring within the particles are characteristics of the absorbing material. Framework FT-IR spectra of SiO 2 , TiO 2 and TiO 2 SiO 2 samples were recorded on Perkin-Elmer Spectrum GX spectrometer.For FT-IR study, the powdered samples were mixed with KBr and compressed into thin disk-shaped pellets with a density of 2.5 mg cm A3 . The framework vibrations of TiO 2 SiO 2 samples were investigated on Laser Raman Spectroscopy.Raman spectra were measured with a Bruker FRA 106 spectrophotometer using Nd:YAG laser with a resolution of 4 cm A1 .The laser power was 200 mW as an output and CaF 2 was used as a beam splitter. The photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide was carried out in an inner irradiation type photoreaction cell (Fig. 1) made of glass with double-walled cooling system to maintain constant temperature throughout the reaction and subjected to irradiation using 1000 W high-pressure mercury vapour lamp with a peak light intensity at 365 nm.The light source was standardized by ferrioxalate actinometry 42.No mass transfer limitations are expected since the catalyst is present as a finely divided free suspension. Prior to illumination, the suspension was stirred and flushed with nitrogen followed by purging carbon dioxide for 30 min and then sealed the reactor under stirring.After irradiation, small aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn by syringe, filtered through Millipore filter membrane and analyzed for formic acid, formaldehyde and methanol.Gas sampling (0.5 ml) was performed at various intervals with a gas-tight syringe through a septum. Formic acid and formaldehyde were quantitatively estimated by Nash's reagent (acetylacetone in ammonium chlorideacetic acid) monitored at 412 nm due to the formation of diacetyldihydrolutidine (DDL).Hydrogen and methane produced in gas phase were determined using gas chromatograph (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column (thermal conductivity detector) and Porapak N column (flame ionization detector), respectively.Helium was used as the carrier gas.Each gas was identified and quantified using the working curve, which had been previously obtained using standard methane and hydrogen gas.Methanol produced in the liquid phase was determined using a gas chromatograph equipped with Porapak N column and an FID.

title: Results and discussion
content: The crystallinity of the prepared samples was examined by XRD analysis.XRD patterns of pure and Ru doped (0.1, 0.3, 0.5 and 1.0 wt) TiO 2 are shown in Fig. 2. The peaks at 2u values of 24.9, 37.6, 47.8, 53.7, 54.9, 62.5, 68.5, 70.1 and 74.88 were identified by comparison with literature data and confirm that the particles are polycrystalline with an anatase structure corresponding to the diffractions of (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), (1 1 6), (2 2 0) and (2 1 5), respectively.It is noteworthy that the diffractograms of all the samples do not present signals ascribed to the presence of doped metal.The absence of peaks pertaining to metal could be attributed to their ultra fine dispersion on TiO 2 particles as very small clusters or due to very low metal content.Also, a slight shift in the 2u values with Ru loading along with a decrease in crystallinity can be observed.Table 1 shows the lattice spacing of photocatalysts with different concentration of Ru.As the concentration of Ru increases, the lattice spacing decreased according to Vegard law 43: the lattice constant of solid solution increased with increasing solute concentration when the radius of solute atom is bigger than that of solvent atom, and it was reversed when the radius of solute atom is smaller than that of solvent atom. The surface morphology of the sample was examined by SEM and Fig. 3 shows a representative SEM micrograph of pure TiO 2 .We can observe spherical aggregates of small sub-particles that are homogeneous in size and shape.As an example, the micrograph of 1.0 wt RuTiO 2 is shown in Fig. 4. Despite the high ruthenium loading (ca. 1 wt), SEM did not illustrate any significant difference between the morphological features of the doped catalysts and those of the bare supports.Even though no evidence for separate Ru phase can be observed from the micrograph even for 1.0 wt RuTiO 2 due to high metal dispersion, Ru particles were directly observed by TEM analysis for 0.5 wt RuTiO 2 as represented in Fig. 5. Small dark spots seen are assumed as Ru particles and seem to be non-uniformly distributed among the TiO 2 particles with a particle size of approximately 13 nm. The light absorption capacity of the photocatalysts influences the efficiency of any photocatalytic reaction.The diffused reflectance spectra of TiO 2 (Fig. 6) showed a large absorption band below 400 nm.The spectrum exhibited a rather narrow absorption band at 212 nm attributed to isolated Ti(IV) species in tetrahedral coordination 44 and the broad band at 330 nm indicates the anatase phase of TiO 2 .The absorption in the UV range is owing to a charge transfer process from the valence band (mainly 2p orbitals) of the oxide ions to the conduction band (t 2g orbitals) of the Ti 4 ions.The band gap was estimated by extrapolating the rising portion of the UV spectrum to the abscissa at zero absorption 45 and the band gap of the TiO 2 is found to be 3.25 eV by extrapolating the absorption onset as shown in the inset of Fig. 6. From the diffuse reflectance spectra shown in Fig. 7, it is obvious that the Ru doped TiO 2 powders show photoabsorption only at wavelength shorter than 420 nm.On the other hand, comparison between the diffuse reflectance spectra of the unmodified and Ru doped TiO 2 showed that the spectra remain almost unchanged after Ru doping with an exception of decrease in absorbance with Ru loading and the absence of detectable Ru phase. Wide-angle XRD patterns of TiO 2 SiO 2 photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 8.The pattern shows that TiO 2 supported on SiO 2 has crystalline anatase phase (2u 25.28).The (1 0 1) reflection due to anatase was found to increase with loading of TiO 2 .Also, the peaks at 2u 38, 48, 54, 55, 63 and 698 became more visible with increase in TiO 2 content. The SEM micrographs of pure silica along with 3, 5 and 10 TiO 2 on SiO 2 are shown in Fig. 9.A distribution of smaller size TiO 2 particles on silica could be seen with higher TiO 2 loading.It is interesting to note that the individual spherical particles of TiO 2 could still be visualized from the SEM pictures.Due to the difference in size between TiO 2 and SiO 2 , several TiO 2 particles are capable of interacting with a single SiO 2 particle.Such a direct contact between the two particles has been found to be crucial for improving the photocatalytic activity of the semiconductor particles. Diffuse reflectance UV-vis spectroscopy was employed to determine the environment of Ti species on the TiO 2 SiO 2 samples.The spectra were collected as total reflectance spectra and displayed in Kubelka-Munk units.In Fig. 10, the strong band observed from 220 to 380 nm indicates the existence of Ti species in octahedral coordination even at lower TiO 2 loadings.Moreover, the absorption edge was shifted to longer wavelengths with increasing Ti content.It is generally known that most of the Ti atoms in a silica-rich sample must form Ti-O-Si linkages rather than Ti-O-Ti linkages.However, with increase in Ti content, Ti domains grow progressively larger on silica support, which is indicated by the shift of the absorption edge to longer wavelengths 46.UV-vis DRS results are in consistent with the results from SEM analysis. The FT-IR spectra of TiO 2 , SiO 2 and TiO 2 SiO 2 (1, 5 and 10) samples are provided in Fig. 11.Pure TiO 2 exhibits OH bending and stretching of water at 1600 and 3420 cm A1 , respectively.In addition, a weak band at 1340 cm A1 corresponds to Ti-O-Ti vibration is observed.The absence of band at 960 cm A1 , pertaining to tetrahedral TiO 2 , is in accordance with the insignificant absorption band below 220 nm observed from UV-vis DRS spectrum.The silica support possesses the symmetrical Si-O-Si stretching vibration at 799 cm A1 47, along with a band at 1084 cm -1 for asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching vibration 48.The pure silica has a very small band at 950 cm A1 , which is assigned to the stretching vibration of free silanol groups at the surface.Moreover, it can also be assigned to asymmetric Si-O-Ti vibration.In the TiO 2 supported on SiO 2 samples, the band at 950 cm A1 does not change noticeably with increasing Ti loading.Unfortunately, the peak for Ti-O-Si is a weak absorption band to be covered by band at 950 cm A1 for silanol groups of SiO 2 .The band at 1340 cm A1 is also not observable in the TiO 2 SiO 2 infrared spectrum due to interference by the intense silica bands.However, a new band at 690 cm A1 is observed for 10 TiO 2 SiO 2 sample, which is a representative of TiO 2 . Raman spectra of 1, 5 and 10 TiO 2 SiO 2 samples are presented in Fig. 12.The samples with 5 and 10 TiO 2 display four modes at 156, 408, 527 and 650 cm A1 49 with an additional very weak shoulder at 208 cm A1 , characteristic of the anatase phase of TiO 2 .The bands at 408 and 527 cm A1 are assigned to B 1g and E 1g modes, respectively, while the band at 650 cm A1 is a doublet of A 2g and B 1g modes 50.The intensity of the Raman bands associated with anatase increases with TiO 2 loadings.Even for 1 TiO 2 SiO 2 , a weak Raman band at 156 cm A1 due to crystalline TiO 2 was still observed, which indicates the presence of some TiO 2 crystallites on SiO 2 .The existence of crystalline TiO 2 as anatase phase on the 5 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 and 10 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 materials is clearly evidenced by the XRD and FT-Raman measurements.A close examination of the Raman bands as shown in the inset of Fig. 12 provides interesting information about the vibrational modes of TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts.The band at 810 cm A1 has been assigned to the symmetrical Si-O-Si stretching mode and the 428-410 cm A1 band to the Si-O-Si bending mode 51.The band at 810 cm A1 shifts downward to 802 cm A1 and becomes less broad, suggesting that some Si-O-Si bridges are affected by the dispersed TiO 2 .Raman band observed at 993 cm A1 for 1 TiO 2 SiO 2 sample shifts downwards from 988 to 978 cm A1 as the TiO 2 loading increases from 5 to 10, which suggests the connection of this band with the Ti species.However, the Raman bands at 978-993 cm A1 is barely observable for the 1 TiO 2 SiO 2 sample, but the band intensity is very strong for the higher loading samples.These bands may be associated with Si-OH groups perturbed by nearby Ti cations.As speculated by Gao et al. 46, this band may be ascribed to Si-OH-Ti bond.Also, a weak band at 1090 and 922 cm A1 observed in 1 TiO 2 SiO 2 has been attributed to silica vibrations perturbed by the presence of TiO 2 on SiO 2 , which are indicative of the formation of Ti-O-Si bond.Even though the formation of Ti-O-Si bond is not distinguishable in FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy results demonstrate the formation of crystalline TiO 2 (anatase) particles as well as the presence of Ti-O-Si bond on the surface of SiO 2 . The photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide in aqueous medium was carried out at a TiO 2 concentration of 0-1.5 g dm A3 in order to optimize the catalyst loading during the irradiation process.In the absence of TiO 2 , there was no formation of photoreduced products, whereas a significant increase in the yield of photoreduced products was observed when increasing the TiO 2 loading up to 1 g dm A3 , as illustrated in Fig. 13.The decrease in the yield of photoreduced products above 1 g dm A3 may be attributed to the scattering effect of light caused by the high concentration of TiO 2 .Further, it may be due to the hindrance of light to reach every particle with the enormous increase of the amount of TiO 2 in liquid carbon dioxide medium.Under dark conditions, the formation of photoreduced products could not be detected, indicating that the reaction proceeds photocatalytically.In all further experiments, a catalyst concentration of 1 g dm A3 was applied irrespective of the modifications exhibited on TiO 2 . In order to improve the efficiency of photocatalytic reaction, Kamat 52 reported the necessity of scavenging one of the charge carriers to accumulate the counter charge carrier within the semiconductor particle.Choi and Hoffmann 53 investigated the efficiency of 2-propanol over other hole scavengers such as methanol and tertbutanol for photoreduction systems.In view of these reasons, 2-propanol was selected as hole scavenger in the present investigation.The effect of 2-propanol with various concentrations in the range of 0-2 M on TiO 2 was investigated and the dominant products obtained were formic acid, formaldehyde, methanol, methane and hydrogen along with acetone formation due to the corresponding oxidation of 2-propanol.The appropriate amounts of the products were recalculated from the electrolyte volume of the reactor cell.The influence of 2-propanol on the yield of photoreduced products is shown in Fig. 14.When the irradiations were performed in the absence of 2-propanol, photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide was not efficient.From the representations, it is clear that the formation of photoreduced products increases with increase in concentration of hole scavenger.However, there is a decrease in the yield of photoreduced products was observed when the concentration exceeds 1 M.It may be due to increased adsorption of 2-propanol on catalytic surface and hinders the adsorption of carbon dioxide, thereby reducing the formation of photoreduced products.Consequently, 1 M of 2propanol was used for all subsequent irradiations. In order to select optimum irradiation time, the effect of irradiation time on the formation of photoreduced products over a period of 1-20 h were investigated and illustrated in Fig. 15.The yield of methanol and methane were negligible or absent during 1 h of irradiation which increased after 2 h to reach maximum at 6 h of irradiation.The yield of formic acid increased sharply and linearly with irradiation time until 6 h, and thereafter the yield turned from an increase to a decrease.The reason for this turn can be attributed to deterioration of photocatalytic activity due to coagulation, diminishment of the adsorption power of the particles and saturation of the adsorption sites on the surface of TiO 2 with intermediate products. From the above results and literature reports, a reaction scheme is proposed for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide with water as follows: Incident photons are absorbed by TiO 2 and photoexcited electrons (e A ) and positive holes (h ) are produced in the catalyst by charge transfer to the excited state of (Ti 3 -O A ).Furthermore, the photoexcited electrons and holes in the lattice are separated and trapped by appropriate sites of TiO 2 to avoid recombination.The interaction of CO 2 molecules with the excited state of (Ti 3 -O A ) lead to the formation of CO 2 A radicals. 2-Propanol which acts as a sacrificial electron donor undergoes oxidation to form acetone by trapping the valence holes that are residing on appropriate sites and simultaneously protons are released.Successive transfer of electron and proton from the catalyst and the reaction medium to stable CO 2 yields radical 1.This reaction is suggested to occur on the surface for effective electron transfer.A competing reaction to this is the reduction of H to H . Instead, hydrogen atoms can combine to yield hydrogen molecule.Besides the formation of hydrogen molecule, hydrogen atom can also be added to radical 1 to yield formic acid.According to Liu et al. 54, CO 2 A anion radicals formed in solvents of high dielectric constant such as water results in weak interaction of the radical with the photocatalyst surface, which leads to the formation of formic acid instead of carbon monoxide.However, solvents with low dielectric constant readily lead to the formation of carbon monoxide due to its strong adsorption on Ti sites of the TiO 2 surface.Formic acid can be adsorbed on catalyst surface to accept successive e A and H transfer to yield a radical 2, which upon reaction with hydrogen atom yields formaldehyde. The reduction product, formaldehyde can be reduced to methanol and the latter to methane as detailed in the mechanism.Based on this mechanism, it can be said that the formation of every product requires the retainment of the previous product for successive reaction. The introduction of transition metals resulted in the change of the electronic environment of TiO 2 .Based on the XRD, SEM and TEM results, Ru metals were adsorbed on the surface of TiO 2 as little island of metal particle that acts as the active sites of the photocatalytic reaction 55 and may favour separating charge carriers efficiently, inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs, and ultimately causing the enhancement of the reactivity.In order to attain optimum Ru dopant level, the target reaction was carried out on Ru doped TiO 2 with dopant level of 0.1-1.0wt and the results are presented in Fig. 16.Formation of all the reduction products increases with Ru doping till 0.5 wt, beyond which the yield decreases.However, the yield of sixand eight-electron reduction products viz., methanol and methane was targeted as primary photoreduced products and illustrated in the figure.The reason attributed to the detrimental activity of Ru loading beyond 0.5 wt is the increase in the recombination rate owing to the decrease in the distance between trapping sites in a particle with the number of dopants.On the contrary, if the Ru content is extremely low, there are fewer trapping sites available, thus reducing the activity.Moreover, the results could suggest that some photocatalytic active sites of the TiO 2 surface are blocked by Ru particles.It can be seen from the figure that there is a significant increase in the yield of methanol compared to bare TiO 2 .The activity of RuTiO 2 was dependent on the doping concentration and 0.5 wt RuTiO 2 has turned out to be the optimum Ru doping level for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide. Fig. 17 shows that photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide with various amount of loaded TiO 2 (1, 3, 5 and 10) on SiO 2 .It clearly demonstrates that the yield of methane increases as the TiO 2 loading increasing from 1 to 10 wt indicating that the reactivity of the highly dispersed TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts is a strong function of the TiO 2 loading.However, 1 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 shows higher yield of methanol than 3 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 on account of the fine dispersion of TiO 2 on SiO 2 as it leads to increased surface-to-volume ratio and increases the chance for electrons and holes to reach the surface for effective photoreduction.Even the lower performance of 1 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 may be assumed to be more efficient than bare TiO 2 due to the much smaller overall TiO 2 content on SiO 2 .The optimal amount of TiO 2 anchored on SiO 2 was found to be 10 wt as shown in the figure, which supports the SEM and FT-Raman results that a large amount of small TiO 2 crystallites present on SiO 2 enhances the catalytic activity.The absence of carbon monoxide as one of the photoreduced product owing to the acidic nature of the support, which is in accordance with the literature report 56. A comparison of the photocatalytic activity of Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 with bare TiO 2 , RuTiO 2 and TiO 2 SiO 2 is shown in Fig. 18.It is obvious that all these surface modification on TiO 2 show different reactivity over photoreduction.It can be observed that in the case of the (0.5 wt) RuTiO 2 , the formation of methanol and methane are promoted by Ru doping.It is noteworthy to compare the yield of methanol obtained from 10 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 with that of bare TiO 2 as 10 wt TiO 2 itself produces methanol yield comparable with that of bare TiO 2 .The increased efficiency may be attributed to the UV transparent property of silica that forbids loss of effectiveness of UV irradiation on TiO 2 , as in the case of bulk TiO 2 36.TiO 2 SiO 2 exhibits higher photocatalytic activity than bare TiO 2 and Ru doped TiO 2 , while the formation of methanol and methane is suppressed with the addition of Ru, even though Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 has absorption in visible region (Fig. 19) with a band gap of 3.04 eV corresponds to absorption at 4.08 nm.The difference between the SiO 2 supported catalysts is attributed to the dispersion and interaction of TiO 2 on SiO 2 .Characterization techniques show that TiO 2 SiO 2 is not the simple mixture of TiO 2 crystallites and SiO 2 but TiO 2 crystallites are interacted with SiO 2 .The interaction between TiO 2 and SiO 2 plays a vital role in improving the photocatalytic performance of TiO 2 .It is well known that metal oxide-support interaction is much weaker with silica support than with other supports 57.Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 may inhibit the interaction between TiO 2 and SiO 2 , thereby reducing its photoefficiency.However, Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 shows better activity than RuTiO 2 .Results have been compared in an attempt to establish the trend.Detailed studies are now underway to access the synergistic effect of dopants on various porous supports.

title: Conclusions
content: From the scrutiny of the above work, the following conclusions can be drawn: (i) Enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of TiO 2 for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide by doping Ru has been achieved.The driving force may be the separation of charge carriers due to the formation of Schottky barrier.(ii) Solid-state dispersion method used for the preparation of TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts perturbs the normal vibration modes of SiO 2 .Both Raman and FT-IR spectroscopies provide complementary and valuable information about the surface structure of TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts.Further, TiO 2 SiO 2 catalysts exhibited quite higher photocatalytic activity than the powdered TiO 2 catalyst as well as RuTiO 2 .High yields observed could be attributed to their high UV transparency and the formation of weak Ti-O-Si linkage.(iii) Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 has detrimental effect on the activity of TiO 2 SiO 2 .This can be attributed to the metal being inhibiting the formation of Ti-O-Si bond. Fig. 1.Schematic representation of photoreactor. Fig. 2. XRD patterns of TiO 2 and RuTiO 2 : (a) TiO 2 , (b) 0.1 wt RuTiO 2 , (c) 0.3 wt RuTiO 2 , (d) 0.5 wt RuTiO 2 and (e) 1.0 wt RuTiO 2 . Fig. 3. SEM micrograph of TiO 2 . Fig. 5. TEM micrograph of 0.5 wt RuTiO 2 . Fig. 7. Diffused reflectance UV-vis spectra of Ru doped TiO 2 . Fig. 9. SEM micrographs of: (a) SiO 2 , (b) 3 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 , (c) 5 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 and (d) 10 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 . Fig. 11.FT-IR spectra of: (a) TiO 2 , (b) SiO 2 , (c) 1 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 , (d) 5 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 and (e) 10 wt TiO 2 SiO 2 . Fig. 13.Influence of catalyst weight on photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide.Fig. 14.Influence of hole scavenger on photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide. Fig. 16.Effect of Ru loading on the yield of methanol and methane for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide. Fig. 17.Effect of TiO 2 loading on the yield of methanol and methane for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide. Fig. 18.Effect of various surface modifications on TiO 2 for photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide. Fig. 19.Diffused reflectance UV-vis spectra of Ru-TiO 2 SiO 2 : a plot of band gap vs. absorbance. The lattice spacing of photocatalysts with Ru concentration One of the author (NRS) wish to acknowledgement University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India for the support of Research Fellowship.
